topics.title: Getting Started with JavaScript
topics.description: Set up your environment and run your very first JavaScript program in the browser and on your computer.

lessons.title: What is JavaScript?
lessons.body: JavaScript is the programming language of the web. Every modern browser can run it, and with Node.js you can also run JavaScript outside the browser — on servers, scripts, and command-line tools. You don't need much to get started: just a browser or a small runtime install.
lessons.code:
// This is JavaScript!
console.log("Hello, world!");

lessons.title: Installing Node.js
lessons.body: Node.js lets you run JavaScript files directly on your computer. Visit https://nodejs.org and download the LTS (Long Term Support) version for your operating system. The installer adds the 'node' command to your terminal. After installing, open a terminal and verify the installation by checking the version.

lessons.title: Running JS in the browser console
lessons.body: Every browser has a built-in JavaScript console. In Chrome or Edge, press F12 (or Ctrl+Shift+J on Windows, Cmd+Option+J on Mac) to open DevTools, then click the Console tab. You can type JavaScript directly and press Enter to run it. This is the fastest way to experiment with small snippets.
lessons.code:
// Type these lines one at a time in the console:
2 + 2
"Hello" + " " + "World"
Math.random()

lessons.title: Running a .js file with Node
lessons.body: Create a file called hello.js in any text editor — VS Code is a popular free choice that offers syntax highlighting and a built-in terminal. Write your code, save the file, then open a terminal in the same folder and run it with the node command. For a deeper look at the language, check out MDN's JavaScript Guide and visit the recommended books section for beginner-friendly resources.

topics.title: Variables: var, let, const
topics.description: Understand the three ways to declare variables in JavaScript and when to use each.

lessons.title: var (avoid in modern JS)
lessons.body: var is function-scoped and hoisted to the top of its scope. It can cause unexpected bugs because you can use a variable before declaring it.
lessons.code:
var x = 10;
console.log(x); // 10

lessons.title: let (block-scoped, reassignable)
lessons.body: let is block-scoped — it only exists inside the { } where it was declared. Use let for variables whose values change.

lessons.title: const (block-scoped, binding is fixed)
lessons.body: const prevents reassigning the binding. For objects and arrays, the contents can still change. Use const by default.

lessons.title: Rule of thumb
lessons.body: Prefer const. Use let when you need to reassign. Avoid var in modern JavaScript. For a deeper dive, check out MDN's guide on Grammar and Types. Don't forget to visit the recommended books section for curated resources.

topics.title: Data Types at a Glance
topics.description: Learn the fundamental data types JavaScript offers and how to identify them using typeof.

lessons.title: The seven primitive types
lessons.body: JavaScript has seven primitive (simple) types: string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, null, and symbol. Primitives are immutable — once created, their value cannot change. When you reassign a variable, you are replacing the value, not modifying it.

lessons.title: The typeof operator
lessons.body: Use typeof to check what type a value is. It returns a string describing the type. This is handy when debugging or when you need to handle different types differently. Note that typeof null returns 'object' — this is a well-known JavaScript quirk, not a feature.
lessons.code:
console.log(typeof "hello"); // "string"
console.log(typeof 42);      // "number"
console.log(typeof true);    // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined); // "undefined"
console.log(typeof null);    // "object" (quirk!)

lessons.title: null vs. undefined
lessons.body: undefined means a variable has been declared but not yet assigned a value. null is a deliberate assignment that represents 'no value.' Think of undefined as 'nothing here yet' and null as 'intentionally empty.' Both are falsy, but they are not the same type.

lessons.title: Objects — the non-primitive
lessons.body: Everything that is not a primitive is an object. Arrays, functions, dates, and regular objects are all objects under the hood. Unlike primitives, objects are mutable and are stored by reference, not by value. To explore data types in depth, see MDN's JavaScript data types and data structures page, and visit the recommended books section for thorough beginner references.
lessons.code:
const colors = ["red", "green"];  // array (object)
const person = { name: "Eve" };   // object

console.log(typeof colors);  // "object"
console.log(typeof person);  // "object"
console.log(Array.isArray(colors)); // true

topics.title: Strings & Template Literals
topics.description: Master creating, combining, and manipulating text in JavaScript with strings and modern template literals.

lessons.title: Creating strings
lessons.body: A string is a sequence of characters wrapped in quotes. You can use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks. Single and double quotes behave identically. Backticks unlock template literals, which we'll cover shortly. Pick one style and be consistent.

lessons.title: Useful string properties and methods
lessons.body: Strings come with built-in properties and methods. The .length property tells you how many characters a string has. Methods like .toUpperCase(), .toLowerCase(), .trim(), .includes(), and .slice() let you inspect and transform strings without changing the original — strings are immutable.
lessons.code:
const msg = "  Hello, JavaScript!  ";

console.log(msg.length);          // 22
console.log(msg.trim());          // "Hello, JavaScript!"
console.log(msg.includes("Java")); // true
console.log(msg.slice(2, 7));     // "Hello"
console.log(msg.toUpperCase());   // "  HELLO, JAVASCRIPT!  "

lessons.title: Template literals & interpolation
lessons.body: Template literals use backticks (`) and allow you to embed expressions inside ${...}. This is called string interpolation. It is far more readable than concatenation with the + operator, especially when you have multiple variables or calculations.

lessons.title: Multi-line strings
lessons.body: Template literals also support multi-line strings naturally — just press Enter inside the backticks. With regular quotes you would need escape characters like backslash-n. This makes template literals ideal for building HTML snippets or formatted output. For a comprehensive look at string handling, read MDN's Text formatting guide and visit the recommended books section for additional learning paths.

topics.title: Numbers & Basic Math
topics.description: Work with JavaScript's number type, arithmetic operators, and the built-in Math object.

lessons.title: One number type to rule them all
lessons.body: Unlike many languages, JavaScript has a single number type that handles both integers and decimals (floating-point). Behind the scenes it uses 64-bit floating point (IEEE 754). You can write integers, decimals, and even scientific notation.

lessons.title: Arithmetic operators
lessons.body: JavaScript supports the standard arithmetic operators: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), remainder (%), and exponentiation (**). The remainder operator is sometimes called modulo and is very useful for checking if a number is even or odd.
lessons.code:
console.log(10 + 3);   // 13
console.log(10 - 3);   // 7
console.log(10 * 3);   // 30
console.log(10 / 3);   // 3.3333...
console.log(10 % 3);   // 1 (remainder)
console.log(2 ** 10);  // 1024 (exponentiation)

lessons.title: The Math object
lessons.body: The built-in Math object provides useful constants and functions for more advanced calculations. You don't need to import anything — Math is always available. Common helpers include Math.round(), Math.floor(), Math.ceil(), Math.max(), Math.min(), Math.abs(), and Math.random().
lessons.code:
console.log(Math.round(4.6));    // 5
console.log(Math.floor(4.9));    // 4
console.log(Math.ceil(4.1));     // 5
console.log(Math.max(1, 5, 3));  // 5
console.log(Math.min(1, 5, 3));  // 1
console.log(Math.abs(-7));       // 7
console.log(Math.random());      // 0 to 0.999...

lessons.title: Watch out for floating-point quirks
lessons.body: Because JavaScript uses floating-point math, you will occasionally see tiny rounding errors. The classic example is 0.1 + 0.2, which does not equal exactly 0.3. When precision matters, multiply to integers first, calculate, then divide back. For the full story on numbers, consult MDN's Numbers and dates guide and visit the recommended books section for beginner-friendly deep dives.
lessons.code:
console.log(0.1 + 0.2);          // 0.30000000000000004
console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3);  // false

// Workaround: use toFixed or integer math
console.log((0.1 + 0.2).toFixed(1)); // "0.3"
console.log((1 + 2) / 10);           // 0.3

topics.title: Booleans & Truthy/Falsy
topics.description: Understand true and false values in JavaScript, including the surprising truthy and falsy rules.

lessons.title: Meet the booleans
lessons.body: A boolean is the simplest data type — it can only be true or false. Booleans are the backbone of decision-making in code. Every if statement, every loop condition, and every comparison ultimately produces a boolean.

lessons.title: Comparison operators produce booleans
lessons.body: Comparisons like ===, !==, <, >, <=, and >= all evaluate to true or false. Always prefer strict equality (===) over loose equality (==). Strict equality checks both value and type, while loose equality can perform surprising type conversions.
lessons.code:
console.log(5 > 3);       // true
console.log(5 === "5");   // false (strict — different types)
console.log(5 == "5");    // true  (loose — coerces type)
console.log(10 !== 10);   // false
console.log(1 < 2);       // true

lessons.title: Truthy and falsy values
lessons.body: In JavaScript, every value has a boolean identity. When used in a boolean context (like an if), values are treated as either truthy or falsy. There are exactly six falsy values: false, 0, '' (empty string), null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else is truthy — including empty arrays and empty objects.

lessons.title: Converting to boolean explicitly
lessons.body: You can convert any value to its boolean equivalent using Boolean() or the double-NOT trick (!!). This is useful when you need a clean true/false rather than a truthy/falsy value. Understanding these conversions will save you from subtle bugs. For a thorough overview, read MDN's Truthy and Falsy reference and visit the recommended books section for foundational JavaScript resources.
lessons.code:
console.log(Boolean(1));        // true
console.log(Boolean(0));        // false
console.log(Boolean("hello"));  // true
console.log(Boolean(""));       // false

// Double-NOT shorthand
console.log(!!"text");  // true
console.log(!!0);       // false

topics.title: Operators & Expressions
topics.description: Learn the essential JavaScript operators — assignment, comparison, logical, and the handy ternary operator.

lessons.title: Assignment operators
lessons.body: The basic assignment operator is =. JavaScript also has compound assignment operators that combine an operation with assignment. These are shorthand — they perform the operation and assign the result in one step. They work with arithmetic and even string concatenation.

lessons.title: Equality: == vs. ===
lessons.body: JavaScript has two equality operators. Loose equality (==) converts types before comparing, which leads to surprising results. Strict equality (===) checks both value and type with no conversion. Always use === and !== to avoid unexpected type coercion bugs.
lessons.code:
console.log(1 == "1");    // true  (loose: string → number)
console.log(1 === "1");   // false (strict: different types)
console.log(null == undefined);  // true  (loose: special rule)
console.log(null === undefined); // false (strict: different types)
console.log(0 == false);  // true  (loose)
console.log(0 === false); // false (strict)

lessons.title: Logical operators
lessons.body: The three logical operators are AND (&&), OR (||), and NOT (!). AND returns the first falsy value or the last value if all are truthy. OR returns the first truthy value or the last value if all are falsy. NOT flips a value's truthiness. These operators short-circuit, meaning they stop evaluating as soon as the result is determined.
lessons.code:
console.log(true && "hello");  // "hello" (both truthy → last value)
console.log(false && "hello"); // false   (short-circuits)
console.log("" || "default");  // "default" (first truthy)
console.log("hi" || "default"); // "hi"

console.log(!true);   // false
console.log(!0);      // true

lessons.title: The ternary operator
lessons.body: The ternary operator is a compact if/else on a single line. The syntax is: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse. It is an expression, so it can be used inside assignments, template literals, or function arguments. Keep ternaries simple — if the logic is complex, use a regular if/else instead. For a complete operator reference, see MDN's Expressions and Operators page and visit the recommended books section for structured learning materials.

topics.title: Conditionals: if, else, switch
topics.description: Control the flow of your programs by making decisions with if statements and switch blocks.

lessons.title: The if statement
lessons.body: The if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is true. The condition goes inside parentheses and the code to execute goes inside curly braces. If the condition is falsy, the block is skipped entirely.

lessons.title: if...else and else if
lessons.body: Add an else block to handle the case when the condition is false. Use else if to test additional conditions in sequence. JavaScript evaluates each condition from top to bottom and runs only the first matching block.

lessons.title: The switch statement
lessons.body: switch is useful when you are comparing a single value against many possible matches. Each case is checked with strict equality (===). Always include break to prevent fall-through, and add a default case to handle unexpected values.

lessons.title: Choosing between if and switch
lessons.body: Use if/else when you need to evaluate different expressions or ranges (like score >= 90). Use switch when you are matching one variable against a set of exact values. Both achieve the same goal — pick the one that makes your code clearer. For more patterns and edge cases, see MDN's control flow documentation and visit the recommended books section for practical examples and exercises.

topics.title: For Loops
topics.description: Repeat actions efficiently using JavaScript's for loop and the modern for...of loop.

lessons.title: The classic for loop
lessons.body: The for loop has three parts separated by semicolons: initialization, condition, and update. The initialization runs once before the loop starts. The condition is checked before each iteration — if false, the loop stops. The update runs after each iteration.

lessons.title: Looping through arrays by index
lessons.body: A common use of the for loop is iterating over an array using its index. The loop counter starts at 0 (arrays are zero-indexed) and runs while it is less than the array's length. This gives you access to both the index and the element.

lessons.title: for...of — the modern way
lessons.body: The for...of loop iterates directly over the values of an iterable (arrays, strings, etc.). You don't need to manage an index — it simply gives you each element one at a time. Use for...of when you don't need the index, and the classic for loop when you do.

lessons.title: Nested for loops
lessons.body: You can place one loop inside another to work with multi-dimensional data or generate combinations. The inner loop completes all its iterations for each single iteration of the outer loop. Be mindful of performance — nested loops multiply the number of iterations. For advanced looping patterns and performance tips, explore MDN's Loops and iteration guide and visit the recommended books section for hands-on practice problems.

topics.title: While & Do-While Loops
topics.description: Learn when to use while and do-while loops, and how to control loop execution with break and continue.

lessons.title: The while loop
lessons.body: A while loop repeats a block of code as long as its condition remains true. The condition is checked before each iteration, so if it starts as false the body never runs. Make sure something inside the loop eventually makes the condition false, or you'll get an infinite loop.

lessons.title: The do...while loop
lessons.body: A do...while loop is like while, but the condition is checked after the body runs. This guarantees the body executes at least once. It is useful when you need to perform an action before deciding whether to repeat it.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.body: The break statement exits a loop immediately, no matter what the condition says. The continue statement skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one. Both work in for, while, and do...while loops.

lessons.title: Avoiding infinite loops
lessons.body: An infinite loop happens when the condition never becomes false. Common causes include forgetting to update the loop variable, or accidentally writing a condition that is always true. If your program freezes, an infinite loop is likely the culprit — in the browser console you can press Ctrl+C or close the tab to stop it. For more loop patterns and best practices, review MDN's Loops and iteration reference and visit the recommended books section for guided exercises.

topics.title: Writing Your First Functions
topics.description: Learn how to create reusable blocks of code with function declarations, expressions, and arrow functions.

lessons.title: Function declarations
lessons.body: A function declaration defines a named, reusable block of code. You declare it with the function keyword, a name, parentheses for parameters, and curly braces for the body. Call the function by its name followed by parentheses. Declared functions are hoisted, meaning you can call them before they appear in your code.

lessons.title: Parameters and return values
lessons.body: Parameters are placeholders listed in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values you pass when calling. The return statement sends a value back to the caller and stops the function. If there is no return, the function returns undefined by default.

lessons.title: Arrow functions
lessons.body: Arrow functions are a shorter syntax introduced in ES6. They use => instead of the function keyword. If the body is a single expression, you can omit the curly braces and the return is implicit. Arrow functions are especially popular for short callbacks and inline logic.

lessons.title: Default parameters
lessons.body: You can give parameters default values that kick in when no argument is passed. This prevents undefined from sneaking into your logic. Defaults are defined with = in the parameter list. For a complete tour of functions, including rest parameters and closures, read MDN's Functions guide and visit the recommended books section for structured practice.

topics.title: Scope & Block Scoping
topics.description: Understand where variables live and why scope matters for writing predictable JavaScript code.

lessons.title: What is scope?
lessons.body: Scope determines where a variable is accessible in your code. Think of scope as a boundary — a variable declared inside a boundary is invisible outside it. JavaScript has three main scope levels: global, function, and block. Understanding scope helps you avoid naming conflicts and unexpected bugs.

lessons.title: Global scope
lessons.body: A variable declared outside any function or block lives in the global scope. It can be accessed from anywhere in your code. While convenient, too many global variables make code hard to maintain and can cause naming collisions. Keep globals to a minimum.

lessons.title: Function scope vs. block scope
lessons.body: Variables declared with var are function-scoped — they are visible throughout the entire function, even before their declaration (due to hoisting). Variables declared with let and const are block-scoped — they exist only within the nearest set of curly braces. Block scoping is more predictable and is one of the main reasons to prefer let and const.

lessons.title: The scope chain
lessons.body: When JavaScript looks up a variable, it starts in the current scope and moves outward through each enclosing scope until it finds the variable or reaches the global scope. This lookup path is called the scope chain. Inner functions can access variables from outer functions, but not the other way around. For a deep understanding of scope, closures, and hoisting, explore MDN's guide on Closures and visit the recommended books section for thorough explanations with visual examples.

topics.title: Introduction to Arrays
topics.description: Store and work with ordered collections of data using JavaScript arrays.

lessons.title: Creating arrays
lessons.body: An array is an ordered list of values. You create one with square brackets and separate items with commas. Arrays can hold any mix of types — strings, numbers, booleans, even other arrays. The .length property tells you how many items the array contains.

lessons.title: Accessing and modifying elements
lessons.body: Array elements are accessed by their index, starting at 0. Use bracket notation to read or update a value at a specific position. Accessing an index that doesn't exist returns undefined rather than an error.

lessons.title: Common array methods
lessons.body: Arrays come with powerful built-in methods. Use push() to add to the end, pop() to remove from the end, unshift() to add to the front, and shift() to remove from the front. The includes() method checks if a value exists, and indexOf() returns its position (or -1 if not found).
lessons.code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3];

nums.push(4);       // [1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.unshift(0);    // [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.pop();         // [0, 1, 2, 3]
nums.shift();       // [1, 2, 3]

console.log(nums.includes(2));  // true
console.log(nums.indexOf(3));   // 2
console.log(nums.indexOf(99));  // -1

lessons.title: Iterating over arrays
lessons.body: You can loop over an array with for, for...of, or the forEach method. forEach calls a function once for each element and is a clean, readable option for simple iterations. As your skills grow, you'll also learn map, filter, and reduce. For a full reference of array methods, consult MDN's Array documentation and visit the recommended books section for exercises that build real muscle memory.

topics.title: Introduction to Objects
topics.description: Learn how to group related data into key-value pairs with JavaScript objects.

lessons.title: Creating objects
lessons.body: An object is a collection of key-value pairs wrapped in curly braces. Keys (also called properties) are strings (or Symbols), and values can be anything — strings, numbers, arrays, even other objects. Objects let you group related data under one name.

lessons.title: Accessing properties
lessons.body: There are two ways to read a property: dot notation and bracket notation. Dot notation is cleaner for known, fixed property names. Bracket notation is required when the key is stored in a variable or contains special characters like spaces.

lessons.title: Adding, updating, and deleting properties
lessons.body: Objects are mutable — you can add new properties, change existing ones, or remove them entirely. Use assignment to add or update. Use the delete operator to remove a property. You can also check whether a property exists using the in operator.

lessons.title: Looping through an object
lessons.body: Use a for...in loop to iterate over an object's keys, or use Object.keys(), Object.values(), and Object.entries() for more control. Object.entries() is especially handy because it gives you both the key and value in each iteration. For more on objects, prototypes, and methods, dive into MDN's Working with Objects guide and visit the recommended books section for project-based learning resources.

topics.title: Debugging with console
topics.description: Go beyond console.log — learn the full suite of console methods to debug your JavaScript faster.

lessons.title: console.log — your best friend
lessons.body: console.log() prints values to the console and is the most common debugging tool. You can pass multiple arguments separated by commas, and they will be printed on the same line. Use it liberally while developing, then remove or reduce logs before shipping code.

lessons.title: console.warn and console.error
lessons.body: console.warn() displays a yellow warning message, and console.error() displays a red error message. They work just like console.log but visually stand out in the browser's DevTools. Use warn for things that are not ideal but not broken, and error for actual failures.

lessons.title: console.table and console.dir
lessons.body: console.table() prints arrays or objects as a formatted table — it makes structured data much easier to scan. console.dir() shows an interactive, expandable tree view of an object's properties. Both are incredibly useful when inspecting complex data during development.

lessons.title: Timing and grouping
lessons.body: console.time() and console.timeEnd() measure how long a block of code takes to run — pass the same label to both. console.group() and console.groupEnd() let you visually nest related logs for cleaner output. Mastering these tools will make your debugging sessions far more productive. For the full list of console methods and advanced debugging techniques, check out MDN's Console API reference and visit the recommended books section for debugging-focused learning resources.

topics.title: Arrays & Array Methods
topics.description: Work with ordered collections using JavaScript arrays and their powerful built-in methods.

lessons.title: Creating arrays
lessons.body: Arrays are ordered lists that can hold any type. They're created with square brackets.
lessons.code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3];
const mixed = [1, "two", true];

lessons.title: map and filter
lessons.body: map transforms every element and returns a new array. filter returns only the elements that pass the test.
lessons.code:
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2);   // [2, 4, 6]
const evens   = [1, 2, 3].filter(n => n % 2 === 0); // [2]

lessons.title: reduce
lessons.body: reduce collapses an array to a single value by applying an accumulator function.
lessons.code: const sum = [1, 2, 3].reduce((acc, n) => acc + n, 0); // 6

lessons.title: Spread and destructuring
lessons.body: Spread (...) copies or merges arrays. Destructuring pulls values into named variables.

topics.title: Closures
topics.description: Learn how closures let inner functions retain access to the outer function's scope.

lessons.title: What is a closure?
lessons.body: A closure is created when a function is defined inside another function and remembers the outer function's variables even after the outer function has returned.

lessons.title: A practical example: counter
lessons.body: The inner function captures the count variable. Each call to the returned function increments the same captured variable.

lessons.title: Closures and loops
lessons.body: A classic gotcha: if you close over a var inside a loop, all closures share the same variable. Use let (block-scoped) to give each iteration its own binding.

topics.title: Event Loop & Microtask Queue
topics.description: Understand how JavaScript's single-threaded event loop, call stack, and task queues work together.

lessons.title: Call stack
lessons.body: JavaScript executes synchronous code on a single call stack — one thing at a time. A function call pushes a frame; returning pops it.

lessons.title: Task queue (macrotasks)
lessons.body: setTimeout and setInterval schedule callbacks in the macrotask queue. The event loop picks one macrotask per loop iteration after the call stack is empty.

lessons.title: Microtask queue
lessons.body: Promise callbacks (.then, .catch, await) go into the microtask queue. All microtasks are drained before the next macrotask runs.

lessons.title: Practical implications
lessons.body: Heavy synchronous work blocks the event loop. Break long tasks with setTimeout(fn, 0) or requestIdleCallback to keep the UI responsive.
